column2: Add wait-for-bugbot job to gates.yml between smoke and the four expensive jobs; chain expensive jobs on its success
column3: The previous gates.yml chained the four expensive Claude-driven jobs (Canon-Code Sync Managed Agent + three runtime probes) behind a free smoke job. That solved the within-AMS race but did NOT solve the parallel-with-Bugbot problem: Cursor Bugbot is a third-party GitHub App that posts a separate check-run named "Cursor Bugbot" from the "Cursor" app, runs in parallel with gates.yml by default, and frequently pushes auto-fix commits 2-5 minutes after PR open. Without chaining behind Bugbot, the expensive AMS jobs fire on the current commit; Bugbot pushes a fix; concurrency cancel-in-progress kills the in-flight audits; new audits start on the fixed commit. The first cycle's Claude tokens are wasted. New wait-for-bugbot job polls the GitHub check-runs API every 20s for up to 10 minutes, succeeds when Bugbot reaches any terminal state (success/neutral/failure/cancelled/skipped/timed_out/action_required), and is needed by all four expensive jobs. Bugbot's specific conclusion is not interpreted because Bugbot reviews different things than the AMS audits (code logic vs canon-code drift) — the gate is "Bugbot is DONE reviewing," not "Bugbot approved."

column2: Proceed-on-timeout if Bugbot does not report within the wait window
column3: Worst case: Bugbot is down, broken, unconfigured, or otherwise silent. After ~10 minutes of polling with no Bugbot check-run reaching terminal state, the wait-for-bugbot job emits ::warning:: and exits 0, allowing the expensive audits to run on the current commit. This is a non-regression: the worst case is "we pay the audit cost we paid every time before PR #92." The alternative (skip audits when Bugbot is silent) would silently degrade AMS audit coverage during Bugbot outages, which is the wrong default for governance gates that the operator depends on. Timeout values: MAX_POLLS=30 × POLL_INTERVAL=20s = 600s total wall-clock with a 12-minute job timeout-minutes (2-minute margin).

column2: wait-for-bugbot fires only when there's actually expensive work to gate
column3: The if: condition on wait-for-bugbot requires draft==false AND at least one path-class match across the three expensive-job scopes (legacy_gate_paths OR canon_or_writings_or_journal OR journal_tsv). If a PR touches only worker code with no canon/journal changes (so the runtime probes would skip on path-class anyway), wait-for-bugbot also skips. Net: zero wasted GH-Actions minutes on PRs that have no expensive jobs to gate in the first place.

column2: The gate does not interpret Bugbot's conclusion
column3: Bugbot may report failure on a real code-logic issue while the AMS canon-code-sync audit passes cleanly (the two reviewers look at different things). Skipping AMS audits when Bugbot fails would conflate reviewer scopes. The gate is purely sequencing: AMS audits wait until Bugbot is done so they audit a stable commit. If Bugbot pushes a fix, cancel-in-progress kills the run; the next run starts fresh on the fix commit and waits for Bugbot again on that.

column2: Validation gap disclosure per canon — this PR's gates run against pre-fix workflow
column3: Per ams://canon/constraints/validators-cannot-self-validate-their-own-fix-prs, this PR modifies a production-bound gate workflow (gates.yml) and cannot exercise the wait-for-bugbot fix on itself. The workflow that runs on this PR is the pre-fix gates.yml from main HEAD, which does NOT have wait-for-bugbot in the graph. Expensive jobs will fire in parallel with Bugbot on this PR as they did on PR #91. The validation event is the first PR opened AFTER this merges. Success on the validation event: wait-for-bugbot appears as a check on the PR; expensive jobs do not start until wait-for-bugbot reports completed; if Bugbot pushes an auto-fix, only one cycle of expensive jobs runs (on the post-fix commit), not two.

column2: No code change in worker/; pure workflow surgery
column3: The fix is exclusively in .github/workflows/gates.yml. No persona profiles changed, no audit-gate runtime changed, no canon docs changed except the journal entry. The wait-for-bugbot job uses standard GitHub Actions primitives (curl + jq + bash polling loop) — no new dependencies, no new secrets, no new scripts. Total surface: ~95 lines added to one workflow file plus this journal.

column2: Post-merge: observe wait-for-bugbot behavior across the next few PRs
column3: On merge, the new gate becomes live for subsequent PRs. Operator can audit by checking PR check-runs UI: wait-for-bugbot should appear, show its polling progress in logs, complete shortly after Cursor Bugbot reaches terminal state, then the four expensive jobs should start. If wait-for-bugbot consistently times out, the poll interval or max polls may need tuning (or Bugbot may be misconfigured on the repo). If wait-for-bugbot consistently succeeds within 2-3 minutes of Bugbot reporting, the gate is working as designed. Worth a second look in a week if PR throughput drops noticeably — but the proceed-on-timeout fallback means worst case is the pre-PR-92 cost, not a hang. Standing flags unchanged: PAT and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in project instructions remain unrotated.
